introSection: Sea Serpent is a steel roller coaster built by Dutch firm Vekoma currently located at Morey's Piers in Wildwood, New Jersey, USA. It is located on Morey's Mariner's Landing Pier.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
The ride opened on June 27, 1984 and was the first Boomerang built by Vekoma to open in the United States.

The ride's train was replaced in 2020.

sections.title: Design
sections.content:
The Sea Serpent consists of a single train with seven cars, capable of carrying 28 passengers. The ride begins when the train is pulled backwards from the station and up the first lift hill by a catchcar. After being released, the train passes through the station, enters a Cobra Roll track element, and then travels through a vertical loop. After being pulled up a second lift hill, the train is released to head backwards through each inversion once more, making the total amount of inversions per ride six.

The logo and station building were designed by R&R Creative Amusement Designs.

sections.title: Incident
sections.content: On July 9, 1984 at around 9pm, the train stalled in the cobra roll element. It had completed the first inversion, but failed to complete the second. The rescue operation required scaffolding to be erected to evacuate riders, and took four hours.

sections.title: External links
sections.content: * Ride page on the park's website

fullText:
Sea Serpent is a steel roller coaster built by Dutch firm Vekoma currently located at Morey's Piers in Wildwood, New Jersey, USA. It is located on Morey's Mariner's Landing Pier.

The ride opened on June 27, 1984 and was the first Boomerang built by Vekoma to open in the United States.

The ride's train was replaced in 2020.

The Sea Serpent consists of a single train with seven cars, capable of carrying 28 passengers. The ride begins when the train is pulled backwards from the station and up the first lift hill by a catchcar. After being released, the train passes through the station, enters a Cobra Roll track element, and then travels through a vertical loop. After being pulled up a second lift hill, the train is released to head backwards through each inversion once more, making the total amount of inversions per ride six.

The logo and station building were designed by R&R Creative Amusement Designs.

On July 9, 1984 at around 9pm, the train stalled in the cobra roll element. It had completed the first inversion, but failed to complete the second. The rescue operation required scaffolding to be erected to evacuate riders, and took four hours.


 * Ride page on the park's website
